mission.title: Install and Configure MinIO on Kubernetes
mission.description: MinIO is a high-performance, S3-compatible object storage server suitable for AI workloads, analytics platforms, log aggregation backends (Loki, Thanos, Mimir), backup targets, and any application that needs an in-cluster S3 endpoint. The recommended Kubernetes install path is the official MinIO Operator, which manages MinIO Tenants as Custom Resources and handles TLS certificates, deployment topology, and tenant-level isolation. This mission walks through installing the Operator, deploying a Tenant with MinIO root credentials and persistent storage, accessing the MinIO Console and S3 API, and the production hardening that real workloads require.

steps.title: Add the MinIO Operator Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the official MinIO Operator Helm chart repository to your local Helm configuration.
```bash
helm repo add minio https://operator.min.io
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install the MinIO Operator
steps.description:
Install the MinIO Operator into a dedicated 'minio-operator' namespace. The Operator owns the Tenant CRD and reconciles MinIO Tenant resources across the cluster.
```bash
helm install minio-operator minio/operator --version 7.1.1 \
  --namespace minio-operator --create-namespace
```

steps.title: Verify the Operator is running
steps.description:
Check that the Operator pods are running and the Tenant CRD is registered.
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace minio-operator
kubectl get crd | grep minio.min.io
```

steps.title: Deploy a MinIO Tenant
steps.description:
Deploy a MinIO Tenant in a separate namespace using the tenant chart. The tenant chart's defaults (4 servers, 4 volumes/server, 10Gi/volume) are sized for a real Kubernetes cluster. For evaluation on a kind or minikube cluster, override to a single-server pool. For production, use the defaults and a fast-storage StorageClass.
```bash
helm install myminio minio/tenant --version 7.1.1 \
  --namespace minio-tenant --create-namespace \
  --set 'tenant.pools[0].servers=1' \
  --set 'tenant.pools[0].name=pool-0' \
  --set 'tenant.pools[0].volumesPerServer=4' \
  --set 'tenant.pools[0].size=1Gi' \
  --set tenant.certificate.requestAutoCert=false
```
The tenant chart auto-generates root MinIO user credentials in a Secret named '<tenant-name>-env-configuration' in the tenant namespace. For production, pre-create this Secret with your own access key and secret key and reference it via 'tenant.configuration.name'.

steps.title: Verify the Tenant pods are running
steps.description:
Check that the Tenant pods come up. The Operator creates one StatefulSet per pool plus a service for the S3 API and another for the MinIO Console.
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace minio-tenant
kubectl get tenants --namespace minio-tenant
kubectl get svc --namespace minio-tenant
```

steps.title: Retrieve the root credentials
steps.description:
Read the root user credentials from the tenant configuration Secret. The chart's default credentials are the well-known values 'minio' and 'minio123' (production deployments must rotate these immediately).
```bash
kubectl get secret myminio-env-configuration --namespace minio-tenant -o jsonpath='{.data.config\.env}' | base64 -d
```
The output contains 'MINIO_ROOT_USER' and 'MINIO_ROOT_PASSWORD' lines. For production, do not rely on the default credentials. Pre-create the configuration Secret with credentials sourced from a secret manager (Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, Sealed Secrets), and pass 'tenant.configuration.name' on install.

steps.title: Access the MinIO Console UI
steps.description:
Set up port forwarding to reach the MinIO Console (the web UI on port 9090).
```bash
kubectl port-forward svc/myminio-console --namespace minio-tenant 9090:9090
```
Open http://localhost:9090 in a browser. Log in with the root credentials from the previous step. From here you can create buckets, manage policies, and browse objects.

steps.title: Access the S3 API endpoint
steps.description:
MinIO exposes an S3-compatible API on port 9000. Forward it locally to test object access with mc (MinIO Client) or aws-cli.
```bash
kubectl port-forward svc/minio --namespace minio-tenant 9000:443
```
With mc:
```bash
mc alias set local http://localhost:9000 <ROOT_USER> <ROOT_PASSWORD>
mc mb local/test-bucket
mc cp /etc/hostname local/test-bucket/
mc ls local/test-bucket
```
Note: when 'tenant.certificate.requestAutoCert=false' is set as in the install step above, the API is HTTP. Production tenants use auto-generated TLS by default and require 'https://'.

uninstall.title: Remove the Tenant
uninstall.description:
Uninstall the Tenant first so the Operator can drain and clean up the underlying StatefulSets.
```bash
helm uninstall myminio --namespace minio-tenant
```

uninstall.title: Clean up persistent volume claims
uninstall.description:
PVCs survive the Tenant Helm uninstall by design (so an accidental delete does not lose data). Delete them explicitly when you are decommissioning the Tenant.
```bash
kubectl delete pvc --all --namespace minio-tenant
```

uninstall.title: Remove the Operator
uninstall.description:
Uninstall the Operator from the 'minio-operator' namespace.
```bash
helm uninstall minio-operator --namespace minio-operator
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and namespaces
uninstall.description:
Delete the MinIO CRDs and both namespaces. Removing the CRDs deletes any remaining Tenant or PolicyBinding resources cluster-wide. The CRDs may have already been removed by the Operator uninstall, in which case the delete commands print 'NotFound' and that is safe to ignore.
```bash
kubectl delete crd tenants.minio.min.io
kubectl delete crd policybindings.sts.min.io
kubectl delete namespace minio-tenant minio-operator
```

upgrade.title: Backup current values and tenant state
upgrade.description:
Export the current Helm values for both releases before upgrading.
```bash
helm get values minio-operator --namespace minio-operator > minio-operator-backup.yaml
helm get values myminio --namespace minio-tenant > minio-tenant-backup.yaml
```
For production tenants, ensure your buckets have a separate backup or replication policy in place. Tenant Helm uninstall does not delete data, but storage outages can.

upgrade.title: Update the Helm repository
upgrade.description:
Refresh the MinIO Operator Helm repository to pick up the latest charts.
```bash
helm repo update
helm search repo minio/operator --versions | head -5
```

upgrade.title: Upgrade the Operator first, then the Tenant
upgrade.description:
Always upgrade the Operator before the Tenant. Tenant CRD schema changes are backward-compatible within a major version, but a new Tenant chart may require a newer Operator.
```bash
helm upgrade minio-operator minio/operator --namespace minio-operator --version 7.1.1 --reuse-values
helm upgrade myminio minio/tenant --namespace minio-tenant --version 7.1.1 --reuse-values
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
Check that both deployments rolled out and Tenant pods are running the new image.
```bash
kubectl rollout status deploy -n minio-operator --timeout=180s
kubectl get pods -n minio-tenant
```

troubleshooting.title: Tenant stuck without pods after install
troubleshooting.description:
If 'kubectl get tenants -n minio-tenant' shows the Tenant resource but no Pods are created, the Operator is either not running or cannot reconcile the Tenant due to webhook or RBAC issues. Check the Operator logs and the Tenant status.
```bash
kubectl logs -n minio-operator deploy/minio-operator --tail=200
kubectl describe tenant myminio -n minio-tenant
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods Pending due to insufficient storage
troubleshooting.description:
The default Tenant requests 4 servers x 4 volumes x 10Gi = 160Gi total. Clusters without a dynamic-provisioning StorageClass that can satisfy this will leave pods Pending. Confirm a StorageClass exists and the cluster has capacity, or override 'tenant.pools[0].size' and 'volumesPerServer' to smaller values for evaluation.
```bash
kubectl get pvc -n minio-tenant
kubectl get storageclass
kubectl describe pod -n minio-tenant -l v1.min.io/tenant=myminio | tail -20
```

troubleshooting.title: Console shows 'invalid credentials'
troubleshooting.description:
If the Console rejects the root credentials, the Secret you read may not match what the Operator wrote. Confirm the Secret name and decode the live value directly.
```bash
kubectl get secret -n minio-tenant
kubectl get secret myminio-env-configuration -n minio-tenant -o jsonpath='{.data.config\.env}' | base64 -d
```

troubleshooting.title: S3 API rejects requests with TLS errors
troubleshooting.description:
By default the Tenant chart enables auto-generated TLS, which means the S3 endpoint is HTTPS with a self-signed cert. Clients must trust the cert or use '--insecure' for testing. The mission's install step disables auto TLS via 'tenant.certificate.requestAutoCert=false' for a simpler bootstrap, but production should keep TLS on and import the cert into the client truststore.
```bash
kubectl get cert -n minio-tenant
mc alias set local https://localhost:9000 ROOT_USER ROOT_PASSWORD --insecure
```

troubleshooting.title: Auto-generated root credentials are not available after a Tenant restart
troubleshooting.description:
The tenant chart ships with the well-known default credentials 'minio' / 'minio123' baked into the auto-created Secret, so unrelated restarts will not lose the credentials, but those defaults are unsuitable for any environment beyond your laptop. If you have rotated them but did not pre-create your Secret externally, a chart upgrade or Tenant deletion may overwrite them with the defaults again. For production, always pre-create and reference an external configuration Secret via 'tenant.configuration.name'.
```bash
kubectl get secret -n minio-tenant
```

troubleshooting.title: ImagePullBackOff on the Operator or Tenant pods
troubleshooting.description:
The Operator and Tenant images live on 'quay.io/minio/operator' and 'quay.io/minio/minio' respectively. If your cluster cannot egress to quay.io, mirror the images to a registry you control and override 'image.repository' in both chart values. The MinIO Helm charts do not depend on the Bitnami legacy registry.
```bash
kubectl describe pod -n minio-operator -l name=minio-operator | grep -A2 Image
kubectl describe pod -n minio-tenant -l v1.min.io/tenant=myminio | grep -A2 Image
```

resolution.summary: A successful production install of MinIO has the Operator pods Running in the 'minio-operator' namespace, a Tenant resource reconciled with all configured pool servers in 'minio-tenant', the auto-generated configuration Secret read once and rotated into your secret manager, the Console reachable on port 9090, and the S3 API reachable on port 9000 (HTTPS with auto-generated certs by default). From there you can create buckets via the Console or via mc, configure access keys and policies for application service accounts, and point downstream consumers (Loki, Thanos, Velero, etc.) at the in-cluster S3 endpoint.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add minio https://operator.min.io
helm repo update

helm install myminio minio/tenant --version 7.1.1 \
  --namespace minio-tenant --create-namespace \
  --set 'tenant.pools[0].servers=1' \
  --set 'tenant.pools[0].volumesPerServer=4' \
  --set 'tenant.pools[0].size=1Gi' \
  --set tenant.certificate.requestAutoCert=false
kubectl get secret myminio-env-configuration --namespace minio-tenant -o jsonpath='{.data.config\.env}' | base64 -d

prerequisites.description: A default StorageClass is required for the Tenant PersistentVolumeClaims. Helm and kubectl must be installed locally. For production, plan capacity (the chart default is 4 servers x 4 volumes x 10Gi = 160Gi per Tenant), pre-create the Tenant configuration Secret with credentials sourced from your secret manager, and keep auto-generated TLS enabled (or import your own certs via cert-manager).
